data.content:
I now have a complete picture of the solution. Here's my analysis and the optimizations I'll implement:

**Issues Found:**
1. **Redundant ProjectReferences** — transitive dependencies are re-stated explicitly, causing extra MSBuild evaluation work
2. **Analyzers/code-style enforcement always on** — `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` + `TreatWarningsAsErrors` slows inner-loop dev builds with no escape hatch
3. **`GenerateDocumentationFile` always on** — unnecessary overhead for local dev builds
4. **Solution has 6 platform configurations (x86/x64/AnyCPU)** but all map to `Any CPU` — unnecessary evaluation overhead
5. **No `-m` (maxcpucount) default** — relies on caller to pass it

Let me implement these optimizations:

arguments.old_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <!-- Documentation generated for every build, even during local dev -->
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Analyzers always on, even for dev inner loop -->
  <PropertyGroup>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
arguments.new_str:
<Project>

    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Only generate documentation in CI/Release builds -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Analyzers and strict enforcement only in CI/Release builds -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">

  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.content: The `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` is still triggering code-style enforcement during build. Let me explicitly disable it for Debug:
data.reasoningText:
The build is still failing because the analyzers are still firing as errors. Let me check what's happening - the `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` condition is on `Release` or `CI=true`, but I see the IDE0160 and other IDE rules are still acting as errors. This is because the .NET SDK has the `AnalysisLevel` set to `latest-recommended` which may set some of these as errors by default, and `TreatWarningsAsErrors` might have a different default behavior.

Wait, let me re-check. The original build (before my changes) also failed with these same errors. The issue is that these IDE rules are configured to be errors somewhere. 

I'm realizing the build is running in Debug mode by default, so my Release/CI condition shouldn't be triggering the analyzers at all - but `AnalysisLevel` set to `latest-recommended` might be overriding that and enforcing certain rules as errors regardless of the configuration. the `.rsp` file is just a build optimization flag and shouldn't be affecting code style analysis. I'm wondering if the .NET SDK version itself is causing this—maybe in SDK 11, `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` is implicitly enabling `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` even when I haven't explicitly set it. Let me try explicitly disabling it to override any SDK defaults.

arguments.new_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Fast inner-loop: skip doc gen and code-style enforcement -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' != 'Release' and '$(CI)' != 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>false</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- CI/Release: full enforcement -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.content: The SDK's `AnalysisLevel` processing overrides my setting. I need to use `Directory.Build.targets` which evaluates after SDK targets:

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <!-- Override after SDK targets process AnalysisLevel -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' != 'Release' and '$(CI)' != 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>false</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

arguments.old_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Fast inner-loop: skip doc gen and code-style enforcement -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' != 'Release' and '$(CI)' != 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>false</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- CI/Release: full enforcement -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
So even in Debug mode, `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is `true`. This must mean that the SDK's `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` is setting `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=true` *after* my props file runs. 

In MSBuild evaluation order:
1. Directory.Build.props
2. SDK .props (implicit imports)
3. .csproj (project file)
4. SDK .targets (implicit imports)
5. Directory.Build.targets

The issue is that the SDK's targets (which process `AnalysisLevel`) run after my .props, and they may be setting `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` based on `AnalysisLevel`. 

In .NET 11, `latest-recommended` likely implicitly enables `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`, so I need to override it after the SDK runs—either by setting it in `Directory.Build.targets`, lowering `AnalysisLevel` for Debug builds, or using `.editorconfig` to control severities instead. The cleanest approach is probably to check what the SDK is actually doing and then override it in the targets file where I have the final say.

data.reasoningText:
The condition is correct, the Configuration is Debug, and the .targets should run... but `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is still `true`. This is very strange. Let me check if maybe my .props is setting it to true AFTER my .targets since the .props has `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=true` in the Release condition which isn't true for Debug... Wait, my .props says:

```xml
<PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>


```

For Debug that condition is false, so this won't apply. And my .targets says:

```xml
<PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' != 'Release' and '$(CI)' != 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>false</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
</PropertyGroup>
```

For Debug that should be true (since Debug != Release and CI != true). But I'm wondering if the evaluation order is the problem here—maybe `Directory.Build.targets` is being imported before the SDK's `.targets` that processes `AnalysisLevel`, which could be overriding my settings.

Actually, thinking through the import order in SDK-style projects: `Sdk.props` comes first, then `Directory.Build.props`, then the project file, then `Sdk.targets`, and finally `Directory.Build.targets`. But the real question is when exactly `AnalysisLevel` gets processed into `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`—if that happens after my `Directory.Build.targets` imports, it could be resetting the value. Maybe I should use the preprocessor flag to see the actual evaluation order and figure out what's happening.

```
Actually, I could try a simpler approach—use `.globalconfig` or set `AnalysisLevel` conditionally instead. Or just use `.editorconfig` to control rule severity, or set `AnalysisLevel` to `none` for Debug and `latest-recommended` for Release. But wait, the original build showed these as errors because `TreatWarningsAsErrors` was on. In my Debug builds, that's not set, so even if `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is true, they should be warnings, not errors. Let me figure out why the original build failed—it was because `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` was unconditionally set somewhere.
```
Looking back at this: my Debug build has `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=true` despite my `.targets` override, but `TreatWarningsAsErrors` isn't set for Debug, so style violations stay as warnings and the build succeeds. That's actually working as intended. I was confused because I wanted `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=false` to skip analysis entirely for speed, but since the build already works, I should just measure the actual performance difference. The real speed gain would come from avoiding the analyzer pass altogether, but my `.targets` override doesn't seem to be sticking—the analyzers are still running in Debug even though the build succeeded at 1.84s earlier.

Let me try a different approach and move the override closer to where `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` gets set by checking the preprocessor output.
